caveats: a: The OEWS table is broad NAICS 522 rather than exact 522180 and reports occupations rather than task time.
a: Current automation through core platforms, rules engines, digital servicing, and workflow software is not directly measured.
a: Privacy, fair-lending, model-risk, cybersecurity, and safety-and-soundness obligations preserve review and accountability work.
rho: Treasury evidence spans the financial sector and may be weighted toward much larger institutions.
rho: OCC's request for information establishes relevance and barriers but does not measure adoption or realized savings.
rho: Vendor-distributed AI can accelerate implementation while creating concentration and operational dependency.
e: The frozen dataset provides no defensible lower-middle-market firm count, so no source anchors the population denominator.
e: Savings institutions include both stock and mutual ownership forms with materially different control pathways.
e: Regulatory approval and capital requirements can make a legally transferable institution economically ineligible.
s5: The FDIC merger rate covers all insured banks and savings institutions rather than exact 522180.
s5: Institution absorption can reflect an affiliate reorganization rather than a qualifying outside control transfer.
s5: One year's merger activity may not represent a five-year credit, rate, and regulatory cycle.
q: Aggregate margin data do not identify the sharing of technology benefits.
q: Interest-rate movements can dominate measured spreads and obscure operating savings.
q: Mutual institutions may direct more savings toward depositor or community benefit than stock institutions.
d5: Deposit and loan balances are financial stocks affected by prices and interest rates, not direct service quantities.
d5: Broad bank-account ownership does not isolate savings institutions from commercial banks or credit unions.
d5: The exact industry's customer and volume trend is not separately observed.
o: Digital self-service can remove labor and branding without removing the regulated institution behind the product.
o: Operator-required demand for depository institutions collectively is higher than for exact 522180 because customers can switch charter types.
o: No source directly measures year-five software-only substitution for this service basket.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Households and businesses place insured deposits and use loans, payments, and account services; institutions earn net interest spread between loans or securities and funding, plus account and transaction fees, subject to capital, liquidity, consumer-protection, and charter supervision.
lens.excluded_activities: Commercial banks, credit unions, nondepository lenders, trust-only entities without ordinary deposit and lending operations, captive finance units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market savings and loan associations, savings banks, private banks, industrial banks, and similar depository institutions providing recurring deposit, mortgage, consumer, commercial-lending, payment, and account services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures broad NAICS 522 credit-intermediation employment and wages rather than task hours for 522180: office and administrative support is 39.76% of employment, business and financial operations 26.16%, and management 11.70%, with large teller, customer-service, loan-clerk, and new-account roles. I map intake, document extraction, servicing, reconciliation, routine underwriting support, member or customer communications, fraud triage, reporting, and compliance drafting to high AI exposure; final credit judgment, relationship management, cash custody, exceptions, model governance, and accountable approvals to partial or low exposure. Broad NAICS 522 includes securities and nondepository activities that may overstate some analytical work, while legacy processes at smaller savings institutions may leave more manual work than the aggregate implies.
caveats: The OEWS table is broad NAICS 522 rather than exact 522180 and reports occupations rather than task time.
Current automation through core platforms, rules engines, digital servicing, and workflow software is not directly measured.
Privacy, fair-lending, model-risk, cybersecurity, and safety-and-soundness obligations preserve review and accountability work.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study of savings institutions by asset size, separating already-automated work from manual lending, servicing, branch, fraud, finance, compliance, and customer-support tasks, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Depository institutions have dense clerical, financial, compliance, and customer-service workflows, but current cores already automate many transactions and high-impact decisions remain subject to human governance and regulation.

caveats: Deposit and loan balances are financial stocks affected by prices and interest rates, not direct service quantities.
Broad bank-account ownership does not isolate savings institutions from commercial banks or credit unions.
The exact industry's customer and volume trend is not separately observed.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry quarterly counts of active deposit accounts, payment transactions, inflation-adjusted loan originations and servicing units, customer cohorts, and constant-fee service revenue by institution size would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Deposit accounts, payments, mortgages, and consumer and commercial credit remain durable services: the Federal Reserve reports that 94% of adults were banked in 2024, while FDIC reports a large continuing base of savings-institution deposits and loans. I expect modest five-year real service growth rather than extrapolating nominal balances, with downside from charter consolidation, fintech and commercial-bank competition, credit contraction, and branch-market weakness and upside from household formation, digital reach, and loan demand.

caveats: The frozen dataset provides no defensible lower-middle-market firm count, so no source anchors the population denominator.
Savings institutions include both stock and mutual ownership forms with materially different control pathways.
Regulatory approval and capital requirements can make a legally transferable institution economically ineligible.
e.change_evidence: An institution-level FDIC and state-regulator file for the relevant economic band identifying stock, mutual, private, and industrial-bank ownership, holding-company structure, charter restrictions, capital condition, control eligibility, and active operating status would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The code is economically coherent around deposit-funded lending and recurring external account service, but eligibility is reduced by mutual ownership, private or unusual charter forms, regulatory capital and approval requirements, geographic or business concentration, and institutions too dependent on a narrow franchise to support a clean control transfer. Stock-form savings institutions are more conventionally transferable; mutual institutions may require conversion or a different merger path.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 5221 ($194,449,056,782) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 5221 ($713,392,970,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 5221. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2102 (margins.json[52]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Financial Svcs. (Non-bank & Insurance) EBITDA/Sales 21.02%. Note: Damodaran reports 0.00% for banks (EBITDA not meaningful); bank codes inherit this non-bank default and are flagged ESTIMATE like all n_band values.); revenue/employee $370,110 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 5221); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Digital self-service can remove labor and branding without removing the regulated institution behind the product.
Operator-required demand for depository institutions collectively is higher than for exact 522180 because customers can switch charter types.
No source directly measures year-five software-only substitution for this service basket.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal customer transaction data attributing deposits, payments, and credit to savings institutions, other bank charters, fintech-fronted sponsor banks, and software-only substitutes, with a legal mapping of the accountable operator for each service, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Mobile banking and embedded or nonbank front ends can replace branch interactions and some institution-branded services, but deposit custody, insurance, lending, payments settlement, consumer protection, capital, liquidity, fraud responsibility, and regulatory reporting continue to require an accountable chartered institution. The discount allows activity to migrate to commercial banks, credit unions, or fintech-sponsored models outside the exact lens.

q.bridge: FDIC reports net interest margin, funding cost, earning-asset yield, and noninterest-income ratios for insured institutions and savings institutions, confirming that revenue is balance-sheet spread and fee based rather than cost-plus labor reimbursement. I translate that billing structure into moderate five-year retention of implemented operating savings, then discount for deposit-rate competition, loan repricing, fee pressure, regulatory expectations, vendor costs, capital needs, and customer switching. All-insured and aggregate savings-institution economics may overstate pricing power of smaller institutions; relationship franchises and locally differentiated deposits may support more retention.
caveats: Aggregate margin data do not identify the sharing of technology benefits.
Interest-rate movements can dominate measured spreads and obscure operating savings.
Mutual institutions may direct more savings toward depositor or community benefit than stock institutions.
q.change_evidence: Institution-level before-and-after evidence linking specific automation deployments to noninterest expense, headcount, vendor expense, deposit pricing, loan spreads, fees, customer attrition, and capital retention over renewals would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: A reduction in processing or service cost is not mechanically passed through contractually, so savings can support earnings and capital, but competitive rates and fees, funding markets, and regulation constrain durable capture.

rho.bridge: Treasury reports increasing AI use across financial services but emphasizes privacy, bias, consumer harm, compliance, cybersecurity, and third-party risks. OCC identifies community-bank digitalization as important for customer demand, revenue, efficiency, and competitiveness while explicitly seeking evidence on implementation barriers and technology-provider arrangements. I translate those sector signals into five-year implementation of exposed 522180 work, discounting for legacy cores, scarce technical staff, vendor concentration, model validation, data quality, integration cost, regulatory examination, and change management. This may overstate adoption at small mutual institutions and understate it when core providers distribute validated tools across many customers.
caveats: Treasury evidence spans the financial sector and may be weighted toward much larger institutions.
OCC's request for information establishes relevance and barriers but does not measure adoption or realized savings.
Vendor-distributed AI can accelerate implementation while creating concentration and operational dependency.
rho.change_evidence: A regulator or core-provider panel measuring production AI use by bank charter, asset size, and workflow, with implementation cost, integration duration, hours avoided, human-review time, errors, incidents, and examination findings, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Document, servicing, fraud, knowledge, and communication use cases have credible paths to production, but lower-middle-market institutions cannot deploy them without intensive vendor, model, security, and compliance controls.

s5.bridge: FDIC reports 136 institutions absorbed by mergers during 2025 against 4,487 institutions at year-end 2024 across all insured commercial banks and savings institutions, an annual gross rate near 3.0%; a repeated-rate five-year transformation is about 14%. I use 0.15 as the center and widen for cycle and cohort variation. The source is broader than 522180 and mergers may be affiliate consolidations or otherwise fail the qualifying-control definition, which can overstate the target rate; stronger consolidation among small savings institutions could make it understate it. OCC confirms regulated bank-merger pathways but not a cohort probability.
caveats: The FDIC merger rate covers all insured banks and savings institutions rather than exact 522180.
Institution absorption can reflect an affiliate reorganization rather than a qualifying outside control transfer.
One year's merger activity may not represent a five-year credit, rate, and regulatory cycle.
s5.change_evidence: Five to ten years of FDIC, OCC, Federal Reserve, and state merger records linked to 522180 charters, ownership form, asset size, affiliate status, consideration, control percentage, approval, withdrawal, and consummation would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Depository-institution consolidation is observable and formal merger pathways are active, but a qualifying external control transfer is narrower than charter absorption and depends on ownership form, approvals, capital, and transaction structure.

principal_driver: Recurring spread-and-fee financial services contain large rule-bound document, servicing, compliance, and customer-support workflows.
principal_weakness: The population anchor is missing and mutual charters, regulatory approvals, capital constraints, and legacy cores narrow both transferability and implementation.

run_meta.title: Savings Institutions and Other Depository Credit Intermediation

sources.supports: Exact-industry scope, including savings and loan associations, savings banks, private banks, industrial banks, and Morris Plans engaged in deposits and lending.

sources.supports: Increasing financial-sector AI use and material privacy, bias, consumer-harm, cybersecurity, compliance, and third-party risks.

sources.supports: Digital technology's relevance to community-bank demand, revenue, efficiency, and competitiveness, and the existence of implementation and technology-provider barriers.
sources.title: OCC Issues Request for Information on Community Bank Digitalization

sources.supports: Savings-institution counts, assets, deposits, income, margins, funding costs, noninterest income, and broader insured-institution merger activity.
sources.title: FDIC Quarterly Banking Profile: Fourth Quarter 2025

sources.supports: Existence and recent regulator actions for insured state-chartered mutual savings banks converting to stock form.
sources.title: Mutual to Stock Conversions

sources.supports: Regulated merger procedures for national banks and federal savings associations and the 2025 restoration of streamlined and expedited review pathways.
sources.title: OCC Issues Interim Final Rule on Bank Mergers

sources.supports: Persistence of bank and credit-union account ownership, credit access, overdraft use, and nonbank financial-service substitution.
sources.title: Report on the Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households in 2024: Banking and Credit

title: Savings Institutions and Other Depository Credit Intermediation
